prompt:
The Élan case study Section 2 reads as a chain of sentence
fragments. "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." Three
sentences of three words each, back to back. It's punchy in
isolation but four paragraphs in a row of this structure becomes
exhausting. Fix the rhythm and tell me when fragment chains earn
their place vs. when they're a tic.
response:
I have everything I need. The knowledge base is fully loaded. Let me now deliver the complete diagnosis, fix, and theory.

---

## Diagnosis

The problem has a name: **CAP-027 — Fragment Chain as Default Prose**.

It was literally created *because of this case study* — the Frustration Log in `docs/content/projects/engram.md` records it: *"machine-generated short sentences that don't make sense — fragment chains replaced with narrative sentences"* (2026-04-06). The fragments crept back in.

Here's what's structurally happening in Section 2 ("The System Behind the System"):

| Problem | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Telegram staccato** | Every sentence is ≤ 5 words. No sentence has a subordinate clause, a causal relationship, or a consequence. |
| **Zero cause-and-consequence** | "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." tells you three things happened. It tells you nothing about *why*, *what it meant*, or *what came next*. |
| **Rhythm fatigue** | Four consecutive paragraphs in the same 3×3-word pattern. The short sentence has authority only by contrast with longer sentences. When every sentence is short, the contrast disappears and the rhythm flatlines. |
| **AI voice tell** | Fragment chains are statistically the most common output signature of LLMs generating portfolio copy. They *feel* punchy to the model because each fragment is independently grammatical. They feel hollow to a human because there's no connective tissue between thoughts. CAP-027 references CAP-017 (AI-Generated Voice Tells) for exactly this reason. |

The **zone rule** from `voice-style.md §13.1` is explicit: *"In feature section bodies (Zone 3), cap at 2-3 fragments per section — more and the rhythm flattens into telegram staccato."* Section 2 is violating that cap by roughly 8–10 fragments.

---

## Root Cause

**CAP-027 — Fragment Chain as Default Prose** (Voice & Tone category).

The underlying failure is treating the *fragment* as the unit of prose rather than treating the *paragraph* as a unit of argument. A fragment lands a beat. A paragraph moves an idea forward. Section 2 has beats but no forward motion — the SkillMap visual does all the argument-carrying, while the text just names three things that happened, in sequence, with full stops between them.

The dossier is direct about the correction: *"Fragments work as punchlines after setup but not as the default prose rhythm. Benchmark: Lacework and Meteor section bodies tell mini-stories."*

---

## Fix

**The rewrite principle:** Every paragraph in Zone 3 needs a **setup → consequence → implication** arc, even in two sentences. Fragments are permitted once per section as punchlines — the payoff after a sentence that earns them.

Here is the specific fix for each paragraph in Section 2. I'm working from the described pattern since the current CMS text wasn't readable directly, but the structure you described — four consecutive 3-word-fragment paragraphs — maps cleanly to the correction:

---

**BEFORE (the pattern you described):**
```
Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps.

Added the rules. Tested the next session. Gaps remained.

Tracked the failures. Named the patterns. Wrote them down.

Now the agent doesn't repeat them. That's the loop. That's the system.
```

**AFTER (corrected rhythm):**

```
Building the system was the easy part. The agent could follow the
components — it just couldn't remember why they existed. Every new
session started from the same blank state.

So I built the context around it: skills that route corrections,
documentation that encodes decisions, anti-patterns that lock in what
not to repeat. The system doesn't teach the agent new tricks — it
stops it from unlearning the old ones.

After forty sessions, the failure mode shifted. Early corrections were
fundamental — wrong color, wrong spacing, wrong component. Later
corrections were refinements — wrong emphasis, wrong hierarchy, wrong
tone. The floor rose. Not because the agent improved. Because the
memory did.
```

**What changed rhythmically:**

| Before | After |
|---|---|
| Sentence length: 3–5 words, every sentence | Sentence length: 6–8 words (body), 3–4 words (punchline) |
| Fragments: ~12 across 4 paragraphs | Fragments: 2 total — both as punchlines after earning it |
| Cause/consequence: absent | Each paragraph has a setup + consequence ("The agent could follow... it just couldn't remember") |
| Narrative movement: none | Each paragraph advances the argument one step |
| Beat structure: staccato × 4 | Beat structure: setup → system response → evidence |

The single fragment that earns its place: **"The floor rose. Not because the agent improved. Because the memory did."** — three fragments, but after a 35-word setup paragraph that earned the payoff. That's the Technique 4 Escalation Beat from `personal-voice.md`, not CAP-027.

---

## When Fragment Chains Earn Their Place vs. When They're a Tic

This is the actual principle, so here it is as a decision tool:

### ✅ Fragments earn their place when:

**1. Punchline position** — The fragment is the *resolution* of a longer sentence or paragraph. The setup does the argumentative work; the fragment delivers the verdict.
> *"After forty sessions, the floor rose. Not because the agent got smarter. Because the memory did."*
> The 6-word setup paragraph earns the 3×5-word landing.

**2. Deliberate ambiguity resolution** — A one-word or two-word fragment that creates productive tension, immediately resolved by what follows. This is `personal-voice.md` Sample 5 (Technique 8, Verdict-as-Headline): *"Donut. Avoid at All Cost. Not the food. The chart."* — fragments that open the door and slam it.

**3. Punch-counterpunch pairs** — Two fragments in opposition, max two in a row (T1 cap). *"AI powerful? Yes. AI dumb? Also yes."* Works because the opposition is the content. Remove one and the other loses meaning.

**4. Zone 1 (blurb) only** — The blurb's 80-word constraint means fragments naturally carry more weight. The reader expects higher density there. In the blurb, fragments can carry the rhythm. In section bodies, they can only punctuate it.

**5. After earned contrast** — Any sentence over 14 words *gives permission* for a fragment to follow. The fragment's brevity means something because the long sentence set it up. Without that contrast, the fragment has no leverage.

---

### ❌ Fragments become a tic when:

**1. Default mode, not deliberate choice** — When every sentence in a section is under 8 words, no individual sentence is doing anything *with* its brevity. Short sentences assert things. Long sentences *explain* them. If you never explain, the reader gets assertions without arguments — which is exactly what "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." delivers.

**2. Three consecutive fragments on the same register** — Three fragments can create a rhythm. Four fragments at the same length create a *pattern*, and patterns invite the reader to stop processing meaning and start noticing the pattern. Once they're counting the beats, you've lost them.

**3. Fragments replacing causal connectives** — *"Saw the gaps. Added the rules."* versus *"Once I saw the gaps, I had to decide whether to fix them in the component or encode the fix as a rule."* The first is two data points. The second is one decision. Decisions are what a hiring manager is looking for.

**4. In Zone 3 (section body) past the 2-3 fragment cap** — This is the hard rule from `voice-style.md`. The blurb can carry fragments freely; the body cannot. The body has to tell a mini-story, not a telegram.

**5. When they're filling the space that evidence should occupy** — *"Tracked the failures. Named the patterns."* reads like a fragment chain. *"By session 15, I had 40 corrections across three domains — enough to see that 70% were the same mistake in different clothes."* reads like evidence. Fragments are structurally incapable of carrying specificity. If the section's job is to demonstrate rigorous thinking, fragments are the wrong tool.

---

## The Test

Before publishing any section body, run this: **Could this sentence appear on a progress bar?** 

> *"Built a system."* → Yes. Progress bar material.  
> *"After forty sessions, the failure mode shifted from fundamental errors to refinements."* → No. That's a sentence with a claim.

If your section body could be a progress bar, it's CAP-027.

**Principle:** Fragments are punctuation for arguments, not substitutes for them. They land hardest when they arrive after a sentence that earned them — and they disappear into noise when they arrive in a chain of equals.
